summary: This iteration represents a genuine breakout from the 6.5 plateau. All six blocking experiments identified in previous reviews have been executed and integrated into the paper: activation patching (0/8 recovery), tightened hedging (6.2% strict vs 98.6% permissive), CMI replication at L0=22 (rho=0.044, null), partial correlation (rho=-0.328, p=0.118), leave-one-out sensitivity (max |delta rho|=0.088), threshold sensitivity (CV=0.077, 20/20 cells show control failure), and control failure diagnosis (23.0% of decoder columns as candidates for a random vector). Data validation (84/85 claims match, 1 missing-data) confirms numerical integrity. The paper now has three well-supported pillars: (1) metric non-transfer with structural explanation (candidate explosion + 0/8 activation patching), (2) L0 phase transition (42.85% to 0.84%, cross-layer CV<10%), and (3) CMI non-replication honestly reported with complete confound control chain. The title, Section 6, and CMI language have been appropriately downgraded from prior overclaiming. Negative results (4/7 hypotheses falsified) remain exemplary. Two issues prevent a score of 8.0: the activation patching sample (n=8) is small and may not generalize, and the confound decomposition narrative requires careful reading to distinguish strict (6.2%) from permissive (98.6%) hedging rates. The two-interpretation framework (Section 7.2) is a mature addition. The paper is now at borderline-accept quality for a top venue.

issues.description: ACTIVATION PATCHING SAMPLE SIZE (n=8). The 0/8 parent recovery result is the paper's strongest causal evidence, but 8 words yield a 95% CI of [0%, 36.9%] for the true recovery rate. A reviewer could argue that 8 words -- 5 of which are high-frequency function-adjacent words (other, often, offer, under, until) -- are not representative of the full false-negative population (186 at L0=82, 656 at L0=22). The wide CI means the data are consistent with up to ~37% competitive exclusion in the population. This is acknowledged in Section 7.5 but the paper's central narrative relies heavily on this result.
issues.suggestion: The most impactful improvement for the next iteration would be scaling activation patching to all 186 FN tokens at L0=82. If logistically infeasible, patching a stratified random sample of 30-50 FN tokens from diverse letters would substantially narrow the CI and strengthen the causal claim. Also discuss the word-frequency confound explicitly: high-frequency words may have unusual encoding because the model dedicates specialized features to them.

issues.description: CONFOUND DECOMPOSITION NARRATIVE COMPLEXITY. The paper presents both permissive (98.6%) and strict (6.2%) hedging rates, which is correct. However, the dramatic difference between these two numbers may confuse readers. The permissive rate (98.6%) appears in the Abstract and Introduction as the headline, while the strict rate (6.2%) appears in Table 4 and Section 4.4. A reader encountering 98.6% first and 6.2% later may struggle to reconcile them. The 93.8% of FN tokens where NONE of the 5 parent-associated latents fire even at L0=176 are neither permissive-hedging nor strict-hedging -- they are 'persistent encoding failures.' This third category is the dominant one but is not clearly named.
issues.suggestion: Consider a three-category decomposition in the narrative: (a) strict hedging (6.2% -- specific parent latents recover), (b) diffuse resolution (92.4% -- tokens stop being FN at high L0 but NOT through parent-specific latents), (c) persistent core (1.4% -- FN at all L0 values). This tripartite framing avoids the misleading contrast between 98.6% and 6.2% and gives each category a clear mechanistic interpretation.

issues.description: SINGLE MODEL FAMILY LIMITATION UNDERSOLD. All experiments use Gemma 2 2B with Gemma Scope JumpReLU SAEs. The control failure, hedging dominance, and L0 phase transition are established on exactly one model. While acknowledged in Section 7.5, the practical impact is understated: all three findings could be specific to Gemma 2 2B's residual stream geometry (d_model=2304) and Gemma Scope's specific JumpReLU training procedure. The candidate explosion argument (23% of decoder columns at cos >= 0.025) depends on d_model; at d_model=768 (GPT-2), the expected fraction would differ. The paper does not compute this comparison.
issues.suggestion: Add a brief analytical calculation: what fraction of decoder columns would a random vector identify at cos >= 0.025 in R^768 vs R^2304? This requires no experiments -- just the concentration-of-measure formula. If the fraction is substantially lower at d_model=768, this explains both why the metric works on GPT-2 and fails on Gemma 2 2B, strengthening the structural explanation.

issues.description: FN COUNT DISCREPANCY (656 vs 657) UNRESOLVED. The paper uses 656 in Table 4 and activation patching sections (from tightened_hedging.json), but the data validation report records 657 FN at L0=22 from confound_decomposition_multi_l0.json. The difference (1 token) arises from vocabulary filtering (1195 vs 1196 tested). While scientifically negligible, a reviewer who cross-checks will notice the inconsistency. The data validation report flags this as MEDIUM severity.
issues.suggestion: Add a footnote in Section 3.2 or 4.4 explaining: 'The FN count is 656 using the 1,196-word vocabulary (tightened hedging protocol) and 657 using the 1,195-word vocabulary (confound decomposition protocol). The difference of 1 token reflects vocabulary filtering criteria and does not affect any conclusion.'

issues.description: LETTER G OUTLIER IN STRICT HEDGING. Letter G accounts for 19/41 strict-hedging cases (46% of all strict hedging from a single letter). The paper notes this in Section 4.4 but does not investigate why G is anomalous. If G has unusual decoder geometry or an unusually strong parent-latent representation, this would provide mechanistic insight.
issues.suggestion: Add 1-2 sentences explaining or speculating about the G anomaly. Check whether G's parent probe has unusually high cosine similarity to specific latents, or whether G's letter frequency or word distribution is distinctive.

issues.description: CMI REPLICATION AT L0=22 USES PILOT DATA. The CMI replication results come from pilots/cmi_replication_l0_22.json (mode: PILOT). While the key result (rho=0.044, p=0.835) is clear, using pilot-mode data for a central finding creates an asymmetry with the L0=82 CMI analysis which uses full data. The dimension sensitivity results (d'=20: rho=0.248, d'=30: rho=0.410, d'=50: rho=0.483) also come from this pilot run.
issues.suggestion: If a full-mode CMI replication has not been run, note in the Methods that the L0=22 analysis used the same k-NN parameters and bootstrap procedure as L0=82 but on the pilot corpus subset. The null result is robust enough that pilot vs full is unlikely to change the conclusion, but transparency is important.

issues.description: THRESHOLD SENSITIVITY TABLE USES DIFFERENT RATES FROM MAIN ANALYSIS. Table 3 in the paper reports rates from 11.81% to 15.10%, while the threshold_sensitivity_summary.json reports rates from 11.81% to 15.10% on 576 tested words. The main analysis at L0=82 uses 1,203 or 1,195 words. The threshold sensitivity data comes from iter_006 with a smaller vocabulary (likely only quality-gated letters). This vocabulary difference is not noted.
issues.suggestion: Add a footnote to Table 3 explaining the vocabulary basis for the threshold sensitivity analysis and why it differs from the main analysis vocabulary.

risks: The n=8 activation patching result, while clean (0/8 recovery, 0/65 control spurious activations), has a 95% CI wide enough to be consistent with substantial competitive exclusion in the broader population. A hostile reviewer could dismiss the causal evidence as underpowered.

The paper argues that L0 is the primary control parameter, but cannot distinguish whether increasing L0 reduces genuine absorption or simply reduces the metric's false-negative rate by improving probe quality. At L0=176, probe F1=1.0 for all letters; at L0=22, probe F1=1.0 because probes are trained at L0=22. The confound between L0, probe quality, and absorption remains partially entangled.

The single-model limitation is real: if the candidate explosion (23% at cos >= 0.025) is specific to d_model=2304, the paper's practical recommendation to 'validate on each new architecture' may be seen as obvious rather than novel.

The CMI section, even honestly downgraded to 'exploratory,' occupies substantial space for a null result. Some reviewers may view it as wasted page budget that could be used for deeper analysis of the positive findings.
evidence_gaps: Activation patching at scale: patching all 186 FN tokens at L0=82 (or a stratified sample of 50) would resolve the central uncertainty about competitive exclusion prevalence.
Analytical calculation of expected candidate fraction at d_model=768 (GPT-2 scale) to explain why the metric works there but fails at d_model=2304.
Investigation of the letter G anomaly in strict hedging (90.5% vs 3.5% for other letters).
Full-mode (not pilot) CMI replication at L0=22 for consistency with the L0=82 analysis.
Cross-model validation: testing the L0 phase transition on Gemma 2 9B or 27B (with available Gemma Scope SAEs) would establish generality.
what_would_raise_score: To reach 8.0 (Accept): (1) Scale activation patching to >= 30 FN tokens from diverse letters, narrowing the CI on competitive exclusion prevalence. If 0/30 show recovery, the CI narrows to [0%, 11.6%], which is decisive. (2) Add the analytical d_model comparison (cos distribution at 768 vs 2304 dimensions) to strengthen the structural explanation. (3) Resolve the 656/657 FN discrepancy with a footnote. (4) Investigate and explain the letter G strict-hedging anomaly. These four actions together would make the paper a clear accept: strong metric audit with causal evidence, clean L0 phase transition, and honestly reported negative CMI results.
